segments.text:
Electrodes and Potentiometry
Potentiometry uses specially-configured galvanic cells to measure voltages that provide chemical information about an analyte.

Potentiometry – What are the electrodes?
Reference Electrode *
half-cell with a fixed composition, so it has a constant potential
includes the half-cell on the left and the salt bridge (indicated by the dotted green box)
Indicator (working) Electrode*
electrode that responds to the analyte activity
the electrical conductor (the Pt wire) that exchanges electrons with the indicator half-cell
*The reference electrode can also refer only to the electrical conductor or wire that exchanges electrons with the reference half-cell. The Indicator electrode can also refer to an entire half-cell.

Where is the solution that is analyzed?
In potentiometry, one end of the salt bridge AND the indicator electrode are dipped into the solution that is analyzed.

Reference Electrode (1 of 2)
The key property of a reference electrode:
To serve as a reference electrode , a half-cell must have a potential (voltage) that remains essentially constant over the course of a measurement.
The reference electrode potential is typically held constant using a saturated solution.
In a saturated solution, the dissolving and precipitating equilibria hold the concentrations of dissolved ions constant.
saturated solution  constant ion concentrations  constant reference electrode potential

Reference Electrode (2 of 2)
The three most important reference electrodes for potentiometry are
Standard Hydrogen Electrode (S.H.E.)
Saturated Silver-Silver Chloride Electrode ( Ag|AgCl )
Saturated Calomel Electrode (S.C.E.)
All these reference electrodes use a saturated solution to hold the potential of the reference electrode constant during potentiometry measurements.

Saturated Hydrogen Electrode (S.H.E.)
The standard reference electrode for tabulated standard reduction potentials.
A standard hydrogen electrode is difficult to use because it requires:
H 2 gas
a freshly prepared catalytic Pt surface, which is easily poisoned
Because they are easier to use, the silver-silver chloride and the calomel reference electrodes are used with much greater frequency in experiments than the S.H.E. electrode.

Silver-Silver Chloride Reference Electrode
The Ag|AgCl reference electrode is based on the half-reaction
Saturated with KCl
Since [Cl − ] > 1 M, the potential of the Ag|AgCl reference electrode differs slightly from the standard potential.
Packaging the Ag|AgCl Reference Electrode

Repackaged Ag|AgCl Reference Electrode
The repackaged Ag|AgCl reference electrode is compact and sealed.
This enables the entire electrode to be dipped into the solution to be analyzed.
Figure 15-3

Saturated Calomel Electrode (S.C.E.)
The calomel reference electrode is based on the half-reaction
Saturated with KCl
Since [Cl − ] > 1 M, the potential of the S.C.E. differs slightly from the standard potential.
Figure 15-5

Voltage Conversions between Different Reference Scales (1 of 2)
Indicator Electrode A has a potential of −0.220 V versus S.H.E.
Figure 15-6
E = 𝐸 ind − 𝐸 ref
versus S.H.E.
E = −0.222 V − 0.000 V = −0.222 V
versus S.C.E.
E = −0.222 V − 0.241 V = −0.461 V
versus Ag|AgCl
E = −0.222 V − 0.197 V = −0.417 V

Voltage Conversions between Different Reference Scales (2 of 2)
Indicator Electrode B has a potential of +0.230 V versus S.H.E.
Figure 15-6
E = 𝐸 ind − 𝐸 ref
versus S.H.E.
E = 0.230 V − 0.000 V = 0.230 V
versus S.C.E.
E = 0.230 V − 0.241 V = −0.011 V
versus Ag|AgCl
E = −0.230 V − 0.197 V = +0.033V

Classes of Indicator Electrodes
Figure 15-7
Two classes of indicator electrode
Metal electrode (shown here)
Develop an electric potential in response to a redox reaction at the metal surface
Ion selective electrode (discussed later)
Selective binding of one type of ion to a membrane generates an electric potential

What is a Junction Potential? (1 of 2)
A junction potential is the voltage difference present at the interface between two dissimilar electrolyte solutions.
Junction potentials develop because oppositely charged ions often have different mobilities .
Example: Cl − ions diffuse faster into water than do Na + ions, causing a charge separation at the boundary between a NaCl salt bridge and water
Figure 15-9

What is a junction potential? (2 of 2)
A junction potential is the voltage difference present at the interface between two dissimilar electrolyte solutions.
The junction potential puts a fundamental limitation on the accuracy of direct potentiometric measurements because we usually do not know the size of the junction potential.
𝐸 = 𝐸 ind − 𝐸 ref + 𝐸 junction

Table 15-1 Mobilities of ions in water at 25°C
KCl salt bridges are used in electrochemical experiments because the similar mobilities of K + and Cl − lead to small junction potentials at the salt bridge interfaces.
Table 15-2 Estimated liquid junction potentials at 25°C

Example: Junction Potential (1 of 3)
A 0.1 M NaCl solution was placed in contact with a 0.1 M NaNO 3 solution.
Which side of the junction is positive?

Example: Junction Potential (2 of 3)
Solution: Because [Na + ] is equal on both sides, there is no net diffusion of Na + across the junction. However, Cl – diffuses into NaNO 3 , and
diffuses into NaCl. The mobility of Cl – is greater than that of
so the NaCl region will be depleted of Cl – faster than the NaNO 3 region will be depleted of
. The NaCl side will become positive, and the NaNO 3 side will become negative.

Ion-Selective Electrodes
Incorporate an ion selective membrane that enable the ISE to respond selectively to one ion.
Generate a reliable junction potential signal (rather than a redox signal)
Figure 15-12

pH Measurement with Glass pH Electrode
The glass electrode used to measure pH is the most common ion-selective electrode.
Figure 15-14

Glass pH Combination Electrode
A typical pH combination electrode consists of the following three components:
inner Ag|AgCl electrode (buffered KCl solution saturated with AgCl)
H + selective glass membrane
outer Ag|AgCl electrode (saturated with both KCl and AgCl)
connected by a porous plug (salt bridge) to the analyte solution
Figure 15-14

Glass pH Combination Electrode Line Diagram
A line diagram for a representative pH electrode as it measures pH
all concentrations are constant except those in the analyte solution
the only analyte that selectively binds to the glass membrane is H +
the two reference electrodes measure the electric potential across the glass membrane

Salt Bridge in the Glass pH Combination Electrode
The outer electrode solution is connected to the analyte solution by a porous salt bridge .
Examples of salt bridges in pH combination electrodes:
ceramic plug (common)
platinum diaphragm (expensive, less likely to clog)
Figure 15-15

Conductive nature of glass membrane
The glass membranes in pH probes must be slightly conductive. Glass membranes in pH probes are built from amorphous silica.
Amorphous silica (Figure 15-16c) is irregularly ordered and has pores that are coated with  negatively charged oxygen atoms.
Monovalent cations like Na + can move sluggishly through the pores (Figure 15-16a), making the amorphous silica slightly conductive.
Figure 15-16
Crystalline silica (Figure 15-6b) is not conductive because it lack pores.

Cross-section of the pH sensing glass membrane
Figure 15-17
Figure 15-18
The two surfaces of the pH sensing glass membrane absorb water to form hydrated gel layers (Figure 15-17)
Metal ions in these hydrated gel regions of the membrane diffuse out of the glass and into solution.
H + can diffuse into the membrane to replace metal ions. The reaction in which H + replaces cations in the glass is an ion-exchange equilibrium (Figure 15-18).
A pH electrode responds selectively to H + because H + is the ion that diffuses most effectively into the hydrated gel layer.

Response of glass electrode
Potential difference across the glass membrane measured by the inner and outer silver-silver chloride reference electrodes
Depends on chloride concentration in each electrode compartment and potential difference across the glass membrane
[Cl − ] is fixed in each compartment and [H + ] is fixed on the inside of the glass membrane
Only variable is the pH of the analyte solution outside the membrane
𝐸 = constant − β(0.059 16)pH(outside)	at 25 °C

Calibrating a Glass pH Electrode
A glass pH electrode must be calibrated:
with a minimum of two standard buffers
one with a pH higher than the sample
one with a pH lower than the sample
at the same temperature as the unknown
The calibration should be repeated every two hours.
Figure 15-19

Storage and Care of a Glass pH Electrode
store in aqueous solution to prevent dehydration of the glass
if the electrode has dried, recondition it in dilute acid for several hours
if the electrode is to be used above pH 9, soak it in a high-pH buffer
If it responds sluggishly, or cannot be calibrated properly:
soak in 6 M HCl
soak in 20 wt % aqueous NH 4 HF 2 for 1 min as a last resort

Causes of Errors in pH Measurement
Standard buffer pH uncertainty (~0.01 pH unit)
Junction potential uncertainty (~0.01 pH unit)
Junction potential drift error
Concentrated sodium error (glass electrodes respond weakly to Na + )
Strong acid error (glass electrodes underestimate the H + concentration in strong acids)
Equilibration time error (when measurements are made too quickly)
Hydration of glass initialization error (when a dry electrode is not properly equilibrated prior to first use)
Temperature error (Difference between temperatures of standard buffers and sample)
Dirty Electrode drift error

The Sodium (or Alkaline) Error
In highly basic NaOH solutions, where [H + ] is very low and [Na + ] is high, the weak electrode response to Na + causes the pH probe to underestimate the true pH of the probe solution.
This pH underestimation problem in highly basic NaOH solutions is called the sodium or alkaline error .
Figure 15-21

Accuracy of glass electrode pH measurements
The most carefully made pH measurements made with a glass electrode have an uncertainty of ± 0.02 pH units at best, which corresponds to ~5% uncertainty in the measured activity, and concentration of H + .
Measurement of pH differences between solutions can be more accurate (uncertainty  ± 0.002 pH unit), but knowledge of the true pH will still be at least an order of magnitude more uncertain.

Table 15-4 Critical care profile
A critically ill patient is wheeled into the emergency room.
The doctor needs blood chemistry information quickly to help her make a diagnosis.
Every analyte in the table can be measured by electrochemistry.

Ion Selective Electrodes in Blood Analysis
Figure 15-22
Method of choice for determining blood levels of
Na +
K +
Ca 2+
Cl −
blood pH
Other ion selective electrodes can measure medicines in blood, such as the anticoagulant drug heparin administered during surgery.

Other Methods in Blood Analysis
Oxygen , glucose , and lactate are measured by amperometry (Ch 17).
Hematocrit , which is the fraction of blood volume consisting of red blood cells, is measured by electrical conductivity .
Hemoglobin is analyzed using spectrophotometry .

Classes of Ion-Selective Electrodes
Glass membranes for H + and certain monovalent cations
Solid-state electrodes based on inorganic crystals or conductive polymers
Liquid-based electrodes using a hydrophobic polymer membrane saturated with a hydrophobic liquid ion exchanger
Compound electrodes with an analyte-selective electrode enclosed by a membrane that separated analytes from other species or that generates analyte in a chemical reaction

Selectivity Coefficient
Ion selective electrodes are not perfectly selective. Interferents can contribute to the overall ISE cell voltage.
The selectivity coefficient gives the relative response to different species with the same charge
The smaller the selectivity coefficient, the less interference by X when measuring A.
The response of an ion-selective electrode to its primary ion (A) and to interfering ions (X) of the same charge is

Box 15-2 Measuring Selectivity Coefficients for an Ion-Selective Electrode
How is a selectivity coefficient determined?
Prepare calibration curves for:
the analyte (Na + )
the expected interferents (K + ,Ca 2+ ,Mg 2+ )
Choose the selectivity coefficient to calculate
Measure ∆ E in a range where the calibration curve slopes are linear and approximately equal (Nernstian Response)

Example: Using the Selectivity Coefficient (1 of 3)
A fluoride ion-selective electrode has a selectivity coefficient
What will be the change in electrode potential when 1.0 × 10 −4 M F − at pH 5.5 is raised to pH 10.5?

Example: Using the Selectivity Coefficient (2 of 3)
Solution: From Equation 15-10, we find the potential with negligible OH – at pH 5.5:
E = constant − 0.059 16 log[1.0 × 10 −4 ] = constant + 236.6 mV
At pH 10.50, [OH − ] = 3.2 × 10 −4 M , so the electrode potential is
E = constant − 0.059 16 log[1.0 × 10 −4 + (0.1)(3.2 × 10 −4 )]
= constant + 229.5 mV
The change is 229.5 − 236.6 = −7.1 mV, which is quite significant. If you didn’t know about the pH change, you would think that the concentration of F – had increased by 32%.
